coredata.dc:description: Interfacing cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) with fluorescent materials provides more possibilities for constructing of sensory/imaging platforms in biomedical applications. In this work, by harnessing the efficient extraction accompanied modification of CNCs and adjustable optical properties of carbon dots (CDs), we report the constructions and emission wavelength tuning of fluorescent CNCs (F-CNCs) composed of CNC nano-scaffolds and CDs. The as-prepared CNCs are densely decorated with citric acid (CA), which plays the role of carbon source for the in-situ synthesis of CDs on CNCs. For the F-CNCs carrying blue, green, and red emissive CDs, ethylenediamine (EDA), urea, and thiourea are the N or N/S sources. Fingerprints of chemical groups, morphological characters, and redox activities are resolved to elaborate the optical mechanisms of CDs with varying emission colors. The emission wavelength is adjusted by either changing the particle size or introducing new emission centers. Both are primarily achieved via precursor engineering. The F-CNCs reveal quantum yields (QYs) >22 % and negligible fluorescence quenching (< 6 %) upon continuous excitation as long as 24 h. Benefited from their cell membrane penetration capability, the F-CNCs with different emission wavelengths were challenged for multiplexed cytoplasm imaging.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation, optical mechanism elaboration, and bio-imaging application of fluorescent cellulose nanocrystals with tunable emission wavelength

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Interfacing cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) with fluorescent materials provides more possibilities for constructing of sensory/imaging platforms in biomedical applications. In this work, by harnessing the efficient extraction accompanied modification of CNCs and adjustable optical properties of carbon dots (CDs), we report the constructions and emission wavelength tuning of fluorescent CNCs (F-CNCs) composed of CNC nano-scaffolds and CDs. The as-prepared CNCs are densely decorated with citric acid (CA), which plays the role of carbon source for the in-situ synthesis of CDs on CNCs. For the F-CNCs carrying blue, green, and red emissive CDs, ethylenediamine (EDA), urea, and thiourea are the N or N/S sources. Fingerprints of chemical groups, morphological characters, and redox activities are resolved to elaborate the optical mechanisms of CDs with varying emission colors. The emission wavelength is adjusted by either changing the particle size or introducing new emission centers. Both are primarily achieved via precursor engineering. The F-CNCs reveal quantum yields (QYs) >22 % and negligible fluorescence quenching (< 6 %) upon continuous excitation as long as 24 h. Benefited from their cell membrane penetration capability, the F-CNCs with different emission wavelengths were challenged for multiplexed cytoplasm imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) are rising star nanomaterials due to their merits, including abundant natural resources, excellent hydrophilicity, high specific surface area, and so forth [1,2]. Moreover, the unique properties such as non-trivial biocompatibility and cell membrane penetration capability enable the biomedical applications of CNCs [3]. It has been witnessed that CNCs were used for devising tissue reparation scaffolds [4], drug delivery systems [5], bio-sensing/imaging systems [6–9], etc. For the CNC-based sensors and probes, signal transduction units are needed to produce detectable sensing/imaging signals. One decade ago, researchers assembled colorimetric moieties such as polypyrrole [10], Schiff-base [11], and Methyl Red [12] with CNCs to construct sensors responsive to humidity, metal ions, and pH, respectively. The sensors exhibited noticeable color changes upon the changes in target concentration/strength. Wang et al. [13] and Bi et al. [14] devised strain and amino acid sensors that outputted electrical signals by assembling CNCs with conductive nanomaterial or Au electrode.

Fluorescent sensing techniques are characterized by higher sensitivity and accuracy, signal-read easiness, and fast detection [15]. Inspired by this, Mohmoud et al. reported the construction of fluorescently labeled CNCs (F-CNCs) by attaching fluorescein isothiocyanate and rhodamine B isothiocyanate for probing the cellular uptake and cytotoxicity of CNCs [6]. Thereafter, a diverse range of F-CNCs was developed by linking/adsorbing/synthesizing fluorophores on the surface of CNCs. The covalent modification usually utilized the reactions between hydroxyl or carboxyl groups of CNCs with the active groups (amino group, thiol group, halogenated groups) of fluorophores [7,16–18]. Therefore, the reactions were selective and dependent upon the chemical structures of both CNCs and fluorophores, which might limit the successful modification. Moreover, additional crosslinking reagents were required for the construction of F-CNCs. The physical adsorption approaches involved the electrostatic/hydrophobic interactions between CNCs and fluorophores, featuring simplicity and cost−/labor-effectiveness. Nevertheless, F-CNCs stabilized by physical interaction were vulnerable to the interferences of complex biological fluidics, leading to structural collapse and diffusions of fluorophores. In-situ synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials is a promising strategy for constructing F-CNCs. On one hand, the impacts on morphology and mono-dispersity of CNCs were negligible. On the other hand, the bonding between CNCs and fluorescent nanomaterials is stable and insensitive to changes in ionic strength or pH. Aiming to explore a CNC-based fluorescent probe for cell imaging, Chen et al. [19] reported a one-pot synthesis of CdS@ZnS quantum dots on the surface of CNCs. The constructed CNC/CdS@ZnS featured long-term colloidal stability and excellent photobleaching resistance. Similarly, Tam's [20] and Gong's groups [21] synthesized CdS and ZnS quantum dots, respectively, on the surface of CNCs. From the perspective of biomedical application, F-CNCs prepared via the in-situ synthesis approach are mainly limited by the unsatisfying quantum yield (QY) of quantum dots synthesized under low temperatures and the cytotoxicity issue brought by heavy metals in quantum dots.

Carbon dots (CDs) are the most appealing fluorescent nanomaterials presently due to their high QYs, low toxicity, ease of synthesis, etc., making them ideal alternatives to conventional organic dyes or semi-conductor quantum dots [22,23]. Moreover, the emission wavelength of CDs can be facilely modulated by controlling the particle diameter [24], introducing molecular emission centers [25], or doping with heteroatoms [26]. The advanced optical, as well as chemical properties of CDs encouraged researchers to conjugate CNCs with CDs. For instance, Guo et al. constructed F-CNCs for living cell imaging by anchoring CDs onto CNCs via carbodiimide coupling [27]. Imea's group constructed a CNC/APTES/folic acid/CDs system for imaging and photodynamic/photothermal treatments of cancer cells [28]. The assembling of CDs was achieved via electrostatic interaction. Noticeably, the reported CNC-CDs nanohybrids were still constructed by post-treatment strategies, either chemically or physically [29,30]. Exploring an approach that can directly synthesize CDs on the surface of CNCs is anticipated to address the limitations of chemical modification or physical adsorption and significantly prompt the biomedical applications of F-CNCs.

Organic acids were extensively used for extracting CNCs from bioresources [31,32]. Simultaneously, organic acids, citric acid (CA) in particular, were the most classic precursors in synthesizing CDs [33,34]. By harnessing the dual function of CA, a successive hydrolysis‑carbonization approach for in-situ synthesis of CDs on CNCs was proposed in our previous work [35]. By hydrolyzing wood pulp with CA, the amorphous region of cellulose was removed, remaining CNCs decorated with dense CA (CNC-CA) via esterification. Subsequently, the CA moieties were partially carbonized to form CDs. The prepared CNC-CDs nanohybrid was challenged to detect metal ions. However, the single-emissive CNC-CDs nanohybrid was not compelling for multi-purpose applications, such as multiplexed sensing/imaging. In this work, by realizing the urgent need to expand the application scope of F-CNCs, we go one step further to tune the emission wavelength of CDs on the surface of CNCs. As shown in Scheme 1 , the CNCs are extracted from bulk cellulosic filter paper, followed by in-situ carbonization of CA to synthesize CDs with varying emission colors. The emission wavelength tuning is achieved by precursor engineering. Ethylenediamine (EDA) and urea are served as N sources to produce blue-emissive CDs (bCDs) and green-emissive CDs (gCDs) on the surface of CNCs, respectively. While thiourea is adopted to synthesize N, S-codoped red-emissive CDs (rCDs). The chemical and morphological properties of F-CNCs with varying emission colors are well-characterized. Moreover, the mechanism of emission wavelength tuning of CDs is elaborated. The biomedical application is exemplified by a multiplexed cytoplasm imaging experiment.

Quantitative filter paper was produced by General Electric Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). CA (citric acid, 99 %) was purchased from J&K Scientific. EDA (ethylenediamine, 99.5 %) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea (99 %) and thiourea (99 %) were supplied by Aladdin. Other chemicals including sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), and potassium chloride (KCl) were acquired from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. Pure water was obtained with a Pall Cascade AN system.

1.5 g of the filter paper was cut into small pieces and homogenized (15,000 rpm, 5 min) with a high-speed dispersion homogenizer (FJ200-SH, HUXI Shanghai) to obtain the cellulose fibers. The fibers were hydrolyzed by CA following the procedure as follows. 40 g of CA and 10 mL of deionized water were added into a one-neck flask, which was heated to 90 °C in an oil bath until the CA was completely dissolved. Afterward, cellulose fibers with a dry weight of 1.2 g were immersed into the CA solution and stirred for 8 h at 110 °C. The hydrolysis was terminated by adding 225 mL of deionized water to the flask. The hydrolysis product was directly carbonized to prepare the F-CNCs (see Section 2.3), or further processed to receive the CNC-CA for characterizations. The excessive CA and other by-products (oligosaccharides, monosaccharides) were removed by filtrating with a 0.22 μm membrane until a neutral filtrate was received. The CNC-CA was mono-dispersed in deionized water (100 mL) by sonication at a frequency of 20 kHz for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of CDs on the CNC surface
ce:section.ce:para: CDs with varying emission wavelengths were in-situ synthesized on the surface of CNCs through a microwave-assisted carbonization approach. For the synthesis of bCDs, 64 mL of the hydrolysis product and 100 mmol of EDA (5.33 mL) were added to a 250 mL flask. A microwave reactor system (XH-MC-1, XIANHU Beijing) was used to heat the mixture to 180 °C at a power of 600 W and a stirring rate of 300 rpm. The carbonization procedure lasted for 10 min. The carbonization product was purified via filtrating through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The solid retentate was washed with deionized water until the filtrate became non-fluorescent. Finally, the retentate was transferred to 100 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated to obtain mono-dispersed CNC-bCDs. After the removal of large aggregates by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min), the CNC-bCDs dispersion was stored at 4 °C for future use. The EDA was replaced by urea (100 mmol, 6 g) and thiourea (100 mmol, 13.5 g) for the preparations of CNC-gCDs and CNC-rCDs, respectively. Other conditions were kept identical to those for the preparation of CNC-bCDs. For elucidating the emission wavelength tuning mechanism, free bCDs, gCDs, and rCDs were also synthesized by the use of CA and varying N or N, S sources in the absence of cellulose. The free CDs were purified by dialysis (MWCO = 500) for 12 h before characterizations.

The TEM images of CNC-CA, and F-CNCs with varying emissions were taken on a JEM-1400 instrument (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 80kV. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the free CDs were acquired on a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL, Japan). All samples were diluted to 0.001 wt% and dropped on the ultrathin carbon-coated grids. After naturally drying, the grids were loaded to acquire the images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements
ce:section.ce:para: For measuring the charge densities and hydrodynamic sizes of the F-CNCs, the samples were dispersed in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) with a concentration of 0.1 wt%. The measurements were conducted on a Zetasizer Nano-ZS particle Instrument (Malvern, UK).

The carboxyl group densities on the surface of CNC-CA or F-CNCs were estimated by conductometric titration. In detail, 10 mL of NaCl solution (10 mM) was added to 100 mL of the dispersions with a CNC-CA or F-CNCs concentration of 0.1 wt%. After acidification of the samples with HCl for 2 h at pH 2.0, NaOH solution (10 mM) was dropwise added and the conductance was continually recorded with a Model 35 conductance meter (YSI Incorporated, USA) until the second inflection was observed. The NaOH volume recorded at the second inflection point was used to calculate the carboxyl group content according to Eq. (1) [36]:
(1)

where C -COOH, C NaOH, and V NaOH represented the carboxyl group content, concentration (mol/L) of NaOH, and consumed volume (mL) of NaOH solution, respectively; m was the mass (g) of CNC-CA or F-CNCs.

The XRD measurements were conducted with a multi-functional horizontal X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). The powder samples of CNC-CA or F-CNCs were obtained by freeze-drying of their as-prepared dispersions, and were analyzed under the conditions as follows: 2θ Bragg angle between 5° and 80°, a scanning rate of 10° per minute. The crystalline indexes (I C) were determined following Eq. (2) [37]:

where I 200 was the maximum intensity at 2θ = 22.5°corresponded to both crystalline and amorphous regions. I am referred to the intensity at 2θ = 18°, which was linked to the amorphous region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS measurements were conducted with the powder samples of CNC-CA or F-CNCs on an AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Survey spectra were collected from 1200 to 0 eV at a pass energy of 160 kV with a scan step of 1.0 eV. High-resolution spectra of C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p were recorded at a pass energy of 40 kV with a scan step of 0.1 eV. The FTIR spectra were obtained on a Vertex 80 V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, German) with the KBr film samples containing 1.5 wt% CNC-CA or F-CNCs powders.

The compositional information of CNC-CA and F-CNCs powders were analyzed on a Regulus 8100 scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi, Japan) coupled with an Ultima Max 170 EDX spectrometer (Oxford, UK). The amounts of C, O, N, and S were measured at the binding energies of 0.28, 0.53, 0.39, and 2.31 KeV, respectively.

The DP values of CNC-CA and F-CNCs were measured by dissolving 0.2 g of the powder samples in 100 mL of cupriethylenediamine solution (0.5 M), further acquiring the viscosity (η) of the samples by use of a capillary viscometer. The viscosity values were subsequently converted to DP values using Eq. (3) [38]:
(3)
DP 0.905
= 0.75
954 log η − 325

The powders of F-CNCs and free CDs were pre-treated in a vacuum drying oven for 6 h at a temperature of 100 °C. Afterwards, 5 mg of each sample was loaded in a TGA209 F1 thermal gravimetric analyzer (Netzsch, German). The temperature was set as 120 °C for 20 min. After that, the samples were heated to 600 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The measurements were performed under the protection of N2.

The redox activities of CDs were examined by CV curve measurements on a CHI-660D electrochemical workstation. Samples of bCDs, gCDs, or rCDs were dispersed in 0.1 M of KCl (supporting electrolyte) with a concentration of 0.1 wt%. Energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbitals (E HOMO) of CDs were estimated according to Eq. (4). The levels of lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (E LUMO) were estimated based on Eq. (5) [39]:
(4)

where in Eq. (4) E ox represented the onset potentials on the oxidation curves, and in Eq. (5) E g was the bandgap width of CDs derived from the Tauc plots of absorption spectra.

The fluorescence tests, as well as the QY measurements, were conducted on an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh instruments, UK) by placing the samples in a 1 cm cuvette. The excitation wavelengths were fixed at 365 nm, 410 nm, and 420 nm for the CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs, respectively. The QYs were estimated according to Eq. (6) [40]:
(6)

where QY st represented the quantum yield of standards (for CNC-bCDs: quinine sulfate, 54 % in 0.1 M of H2SO4 solution; for CNC-gCDs: rhodamine 6G, 95 % in ethanol; for CNC-rCDs: rhodamine B, 89 % in ethanol), F and F st were the fluorescence profile integrals of F-CNCs and standards, respectively. A st and A were the absorption intensities of standards and F-CNCs, respectively. n w and n st represented the refraction index of water (1.33) and media for dissolving the standards, respectively.

The transient fluorescence measurements were performed on an FLS980 Series of Fluorescence Spectrophotometers (Edinburgh instruments). The scanning step was fixed at 0.2 ns, and the excitation wavelengths were 365 nm, 410 nm, and 420 nm for CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs, respectively.

HeLa cells were used as the model to examine the cytotoxicity and cytoplasm imaging ability of the F-CNCs. The cells were cultured in 10 % FBS-supplemented DMEM at 37 °C with a 5 % CO2-humidified atmosphere. Cytotoxicity of the F-CNCs was evaluated by MTT assay [41]. For the cytoplasm imaging experiments, the cells were incubated for 24 h, followed by the addition of CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, or CNC-rCDs to the culture media. The final concentration was kept at 0.05 mg/mL for the F-CNCs. After further incubation for 4 h, the cells were washed with PBS buffer three times and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution. The bright field images, coupled with the fluorescent images of the cells were taken on an LSM710 laser confocal microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

In this work, the F-CNCs are prepared from bulk cellulose resource (filter paper) following a successive hydrolysis‑carbonization procedure. The filter paper was first homogenized to receive cellulose fibers. At elevated temperatures, the amorphous region of cellulose fibers is facilely degraded by H+, thus remaining CNCs. The concurrent esterification between cellulose and CA results in the modification of CNCs with a high density of CA molecules, which renders CNCs with satisfying mono-dispersity and, more importantly, provides the carbon source for in-situ synthesis of CDs. Quantitatively, the carboxyl content of CNC-CA is 1.79 ± 0.13 mmol/g (Table S1 in the Supplementary Materials), obviously higher than the CNCs prepared via the TEMPO method [42]. The corresponding substitution degree is calculated as 0.35 ± 0.03 following the reported method [43]. The high CA concentration employed to digest cellulose fibers is considered to give rise to the efficient modification of CNCs. Upon carbonization, dehydration of CA molecules occurs, and in-situ synthesis of CDs is thus achievable. As shown in Fig. 1a, the concentration of the as-prepared CNC-CA colloidal dispersion is estimated as 1.56 ± 0.18 mg/mL. After carbonization, the suspensions become light-yellow, yellow-green, and yellow for CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs, respectively, with slight changes in the concentrations (CNC-bCDs: 1.54 ± 0.18 mg/mL; CNC-gCDs: 1.57 ± 0.11 mg/mL; CNC-rCDs: 1.43 ± 0. 17 mg/mL). The unchanged emission intensities of F-CNCs dialyzed in different media, as well as precipitated by centrifugation (15,000 rpm, 30 min), suggest the stable covalent bonding between CDs and CNC backbones (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Materials). Relying on the different thermo-degradation temperatures of CDs and CNC backbones (Fig. S2), the amounts of bCDs, gCDs, and rCDs are quantified as 6.47, 6.45, and 6.83 wt% in the F-CNCs with corresponding emission colors. The TEM images reveal that the CNC-CA and F-CNCs possess similar morphologies, but the uniform distribution of CDs is observable for F-CNCs (Fig. 1b). The statistical (length, width) are (207 ± 52 nm, 25 ± 4.5 nm), (215 ± 43 nm, 25 ± 5.7 nm), (210 ± 68 nm, 27 ± 7.1 nm), and (223 ± 56 nm, 27 ± 5.0 nm) for CNC-CA, CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs, respectively. The length scales are in good consistency with the hydrodynamic sizes (Fig. S3 in the Supplementary Materials), implying excellent mono-dispersity of both CNC-CA and F-CNCs. The Zeta potential measurements demonstrate a decrement in surface charge density for the CNCs experienced carbonization. The decrement is attributed to the loss of carboxyl groups (Table S1 in the Supplementary Materials) after carbonization and the neutralization effect of amino groups introduced by N sources. These results can verify the facile preparations of F-CNCs by in-situ synthesis of CDs via a one-pot hydrolysis‑carbonization strategy.

In the F-CNCs, whether the CNC backbone participates in the formation of CDs or only provides a substrate for CDs is the first question to be addressed. XRD measurements were thus performed to clarify the roles of CNCs played in the nanohybrids. The CNC-CA, CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs all exhibit four prominent diffraction peaks at 14.9°, 16.5°, 22.7°, and 34.5°, corresponding to the (1̅10), (110), (200), and (004) planes of type I cellulose crystal (Fig. S4). According to the formula, the crystalline indexes are 75 ± 0.8 %, 79 ± 1.3 %, 76 ± 0.9 %, and 75 ± 0.9 %, respectively. The similarities in XRD patterns and crystalline indexes indicate that CNCs are not involved in the carbonization except for serving as the nanoscaffold. In parallel with the crystallinity, the DP values of F-CNCs are close to that of CNC-CA (Table S2), confirming the negligible degradation of CNCs during the synthesis of CDs.

CDs' emission characters, including QY and emission wavelength, largely rely on their chemical properties. The F-CNCs were thus subjected to FTIR and XPS measurements for parsing the chemical groups. As shown in Fig. 2a, in the spectrum of CNC-CA, the characteristic peaks at 3300–3500 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, and 1050 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups, stretching vibration of CO bonds in carboxyl groups, and antisymmetric vibration of C-O-C structure in glucose units of cellulose, respectively. The emergence of the carboxyl group evidences the successful modification of CA via esterification. After carbonization, new peaks arise in the spectra of F-CNCs. Particularly, the peak at 1740 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of the NH bond indicates the successful introduction of amino groups in CDs. For CNC-gCDs and CNC-rCDs, carbonizations of urea and thiourea, respectively, with CA lead to the emergences of vibrations of conjugated CN or CS groups (2100 cm−1) in their spectra [44]. Besides, the weak peak at 2510 cm−1 in the spectrum of CNC-rCDs is attributed to the stretching vibration of the SH bond, confirming that rCDs are codoped with N and S. The EDX mapping results also demonstrate the successive appearances of N in CNC-bCDs and CNC-gCDs, and N, S in CNC-rCDs (Fig. S5). The elemental contents in CNC-CA and F-CNCs can be seen in Table S3. Although the amounts of doped N and S are small, they play the vital roles in improving the QYs and tuning the emission wavelength of CDs, which will be discussed in Section 3.4.

The XPS measurements can resolve the bonding states of atoms on F-CNCs. The full spectrum survey reveals the successful doping of N for bCDs and gCDs and N, S-codoping for rCDs due to the appearances of N1s signal or N1s/S2p signals on the corresponding spectrum (Fig. S6). The high-resolution C1s spectra can emphasize the CA modification of CNCs by showing the CO bond at 287.9 eV (Fig. 2b). After carbonization, the CN bond (286.5 eV) and CN bond (288.2 eV) appear in the spectra of F-CNCs (Fig. 2c-e). Interestingly, the peak areas of C=O/C=N groups in samples of CNC-gCDs and C=O/C=N/C=S groups in CNC-rCDs are greater than that in CNC-bCDs. Given that identical CNC-CA substrates are employed to in-situ synthesize CDs, the amount of carbonyl groups is unlikely to increase. Therefore, increments in the CN group or C=N/C=S groups are the reasons for this phenomenon. This speculation can be evidenced by the improved N concentration in CNC-gCDs and (N + S) concentration in CNC-rCDs revealed by both the EDX and XPS measurements (Table S3). The N1s spectra are resolved into three peaks at 389.9, 400.4, and 401.7 eV, which are assigned to the pyridinic NC, NH, and graphitic NC3 bonds, respectively (Fig. 2f-h). These results suggest that N atoms can be doped in the central region of sp2-hybridized graphitic lattice (forming NC3), on the edges of carbon lattice (forming pyridinic CN), or embedded in the amorphous part of CDs as unconjugated amino groups. The peak fitting result of S2p signal (Fig. 2i) in rCDs reveals the presence of thiophene S (SC or SC) at 164.3 eV and C-SOx at 166.1 eV [44]. The FTIR and XPS results prove that the F-CNCs are rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Amino groups can be found in CNC-bCDs and CNC-gCDs due to the co‑carbonizations of EDA or urea with CA, while amino and thiol groups are observable in CNC-rCDs since thiourea is used as N, S source. Importantly, conjugated groups such as graphitic NC3, CO, CN, or thiophene S are considered to produce significant impacts on the optical properties of F-CNCs with varying emission wavelengths.

Biomedical applications of F-CNCs require satisfying brightness and photo-stability. Benefited from the optical inertness and transparency of CNCs, the fluorescence of CDs cannot be influenced by CNCs via electron or energy transfer. Therefore, the optical properties of F-CNCs are reflected by the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3a, CNC-CA shows no extinction peaks in the UV–vis region. In-situ synthesis of CDs renders the F-CNCs with absorption characters at varying wavelengths. For CNC-bCDs, the maximum extinction occurs at 338 nm, which is stemmed from the n-π* transition of CO and CN groups [45]. For CNC-gCDs and CNC-rCDs, the peaks redshift to 420 nm and 425 nm, respectively, implying an extended π-domain that is composed of sp2 conjugated chromophores carrying CO, CN, or CS bonds [46,47]. The result agrees well with the findings of XPS measurements. For revealing their emission properties, the F-CNCs were subjected to irradiations at different excitation wavelengths. The CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs were excited by the lights ranging from 300 to 370 nm, 350 to 420 nm, and 400 to 470 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3b-d, the emissions of CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs are located at 460 nm, 506 nm, and 645 nm, respectively, and are excitation-independent. The excitation-independence suggests that the bCDs, gCDs, and rCDs possess uniform size distributions and surface states to provide emissions with identical energies at different excitation wavelengths [48]. Under optimal excitations, the CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs emit bright blue, green, and red fluorescence with the estimated QYs of 46.1 ± 1.5 %, 26.7 ± 1.9 %, and 22.3 ± 1.4 %, respectively, which are comparable to those F-CNCs reported elsewhere (Table S4). In five independent batches of preparations, the F-CNCs exhibit negligible fluctuations in the emission wavelengths and the QYs (Fig. S7), indicative of the reproducible F-CNCs preparations. Unless being co‑carbonized with EDA, urea, or thiourea, the carbonization product of sole CNC-CA is non-fluorescent (Fig. S8), suggesting the necessity of N, or N, S-dopings in improving the QYs of CDs. The photo-stability was assessed by continuously exposing the F-CNCs to excitation lights. The fluorescence quenching is 4.4 %, 5.3 %, and 5.1 % for CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs under 24 h excitation (Fig. S9). Besides, the fluorescence intensities of F-CNCs reveal slight changes in the pH range of 4.0 to 11.0 (Fig. S10). The desirable optical stability is considered to be pivotal for the long-term bio-imaging application in complex biological media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the emission wavelength tuning
ce:section.ce:para: Figuring out the mechanisms behind the CDs' optical behaviors is of great significance for customizing CD-based fluorescent materials with desirable emission characters. In this work, CDs with blue, green, and red emissions are facilely synthesized on the surface of CNCs by precursor engineering. For elaborating the emission wavelength tuning mechanism, the free bCDs, gCDs, and rCDs were characterized by high-resolution TEM and CV curve measurements. As can be seen in Fig. 4a-f, all CDs are spherical-shaped with definite crystalline carbon cores. The HRTEM images show that the crystalline lattice space is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon [49]. The highly ordered graphite structure of these CDs is associated with their excitation-independent emission property. Noticeably, the average diameters of bCDs, gCDs, and rCDs are 2.76 nm, 3.72 nm, and 3.98 nm, respectively (Fig. 4g-i). Simultaneously, the crystalline π-domain gets extended for gCDs and rCDs compared to bCDs. The result of the XPS analysis is further confirmed by these HRTEM images. As such, we speculate that the bandgap emission is dominant for bCDs and gCDs since the quantum size effect (QSE) produces the most remarkable impact on emission wavelength.

For understanding the electron transition pathway in CDs, the LUMO and HOMO levels of the CDs were estimated according to their oxidation potentials in CV curves and Tauc plots derived from absorption spectra (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. 5a and b, the bandgap widths (E g) for bCDs and gCDs are 3.11 eV and 2.55 eV, respectively. Further, according to the oxidation onset potentials shown in Fig. 5d and e, HOMO levels of bCDs and gCDs are estimated as −5.58 eV and − 5.08 eV, respectively. The LUMO levels are thus derived with the known E g (Fig. 5g). From the chemical perspective, the precursors are first polymerized and further carbonized to form CDs [50,51]. Compared to linear EDA molecules, polymerization of urea molecules with CA is assumed to generate hyperbranched intermediates that are carbonized to create CDs with larger particle sizes and extended π-domain. As a consequence, the replacement of EDA by equivalent urea leads to the E g decrement and emission wavelength redshift of CDs. The hypothesis can be verified by the morphological details of rCDs synthesized by CA and thiourea. The highly structural consistency of thiourea and urea leads to the similarities in π-domain sizes and particle diameters of gCDs and rCDs. The bandgap width for rCDs is determined as 2.50 eV (Fig. 5c). In combination with the onset potential of oxidation (0.5 eV) for rCDs (Fig. 5f), the HOMO and LUMO levels are thus estimated. Noticeably, both the bandgap width and energy levels of rCDs are comparable to those of gCDs. Nevertheless, the emission wavelength of rCDs is largely redshifted to 645 nm. The distinguishable emission character implies that additional emission centers must be introduced within rCDs. The emission centers can emit fluorescence with lower energy than the bandgap exciton recombination. In the compositional analysis, it has been proved that, beyond N-containing groups, the N, S-codoped rCDs possess S-containing groups such as thiophene or thiol. As is evidenced in the earlier reports [52–54], the contribution of doped heteroatoms to the electronic structure of CDs is quite different. For N, its three valence electrons form three δ-bonds with the neighbouring C, and the other two electrons are engaged in π-bond formation. The π-system of CDs remains integrated, and the bandgap is not obviously changed upon the sole N-doping. In contrast, the S atom possesses larger radius and more valence electrons. As a consequence, the S dopant can give rise to the locally geometric deformations of graphitic C and reduce the sp2 hybridization, introducing the in-gap trap state and reducing bandgap. The effectiveness of S in lowering the bandgap of CDs is also evidenced in the CDs for metal ion sensing [55] or photoelectrical conversion [56]. For our case, the efficient transfer of excited electrons from the LUMO to the energy bands of trap states occurs in rCDs, followed by the radiative recombination and release of photons with longer wavelengths. Meanwhile, in comparison with those of bCDs and gCDs, novel reduction potential can be found in the CV curve of rCDs (Fig. S12), indicative of the insertion of in-gap trap states. Besides, the fluorescence lifetimes gradually decrease from bCDs (14.01 ns) to gCDs (8.58 ns) and then to rCDs (5.77 ns) (Fig. S13), declaring the shortened radiative exciton recombination paths along with the emission redshift of the CDs [57,58]. Together, we can conclude that two mechanisms are involved in adjusting the emission wavelength of CDs. For bCDs and gCDs, the redshift is attributed to the QSE, whilst N, S-codoping induced trap state emission dominates the wavelength shift from green to red.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiplexed cytoplasm imaging with the F-CNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the proposed F-CNCs in “lighting-up” biological objectives was evaluated by the cytoplasm imaging experiment. The MTT assay reveals that the cell viability is >80 % after 10 h of co-incubation of the cells with 0.1 mg/mL of F-CNCs (Fig. S14). The negligible cytotoxicity is ascribed to the excellent biocompatibility of both CNCs and CDs. In the cytoplasm imaging experiment, the concentration of CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs was kept at 0.05 mg/mL, and the co-incubation time was 4 h. As shown in Fig. 6 , bright blue, green, and red fluorescence are observed in the fluorescent channels of cells treated with CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs, respectively. The overlay images show that the fluorescent areas are constrained in cytoplasm instead of nuclei. The single-cell emission intensity analysis (by counting 50 cells) declares acceptable differences in the uptake of F-CNCs. The RSD values are 10.8 %, 11.8 %, and 8.5 % for CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs treated cells, respectively. These results demonstrate that the F-CNCs can penetrate cell membranes and “light-up” cytoplasm. More importantly, their structural stability inhibits the diffusions of CDs into the cell nucleus, contributing to the imaging specificity. The optical stability of F-CNCs prevents the fluorescence quenching in complex biological environments. Therefore, the signal-read easiness is achieved by the non-trivial signal-to-noise ratio.

By advancing the emission wavelength tuning during the in-situ synthesis of CDs, unconventional F-CNCs with multiple emission colors are fabricated, characterized, and applied in bio-imaging. The F-CNCs are prepared following a hydrolysis‑carbonization procedure, during which the emission wavelength of CDs synthesized on the surface of CNCs is adjusted by precursor engineering. CA is consistently served as the carbon source, while the co‑carbonized N or N, S sources are EDA, urea, and thiourea for the synthesis of CNC-bCDs, CNC-gCDs, and CNC-rCDs, respectively. The mechanism analysis reveals that the main reason is QSE for the emission wavelength tuning from blue to green, while alternation of bandgap emission by trap state emission is responsible for the emission color change from green to red. Featuring excellent QYs, outperforming optical/structural stabilities, as well as negligible cytotoxicity, the F-CNCs are successfully applied in multiplexed and specific imaging of cytoplasm. Based on these advanced properties, in combination with the emission wavelength tuning facility, it is anticipated that multi-purpose applications of the proposed F-CNCs in quantifying biomarkers or monitoring physiological/pathological events can be achieved by appropriate functionalization.
This work did not involve the use of human subjects and the animal experiment.
